title: Pranayama During Kundalini Crisis: Which Breathwork Helps and Which Harms
meta_description: You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, scrolling in the dark, heart hammering, mind racing, feeling like you're losing it. I know that feeling. I've been
body:
Pranayama During Kundalini Crisis: Which Breathwork Helps and Which Harms

You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, scrolling in the dark, heart hammering, mind racing, feeling like you're losing it. I know that feeling. I've been there, staring at the ceiling, feeling electric currents run through me, hearing things, seeing things, convinced I'd broken my brain for good. You're looking for answers about breathwork, about pranayama, because someone, somewhere, told you it could help. Or maybe it's what got you into this mess in the first place. Either way, you're desperate to know what's safe and what's not when your whole system feels like it's short-circuiting.

Let's get one thing straight right away: this isn't some abstract discussion for me. This is my life. I went through it. The terror, the confusion, the feeling of being utterly alone in a reality that had twisted itself into something unrecognizable. So, when I talk about kundalini crisis, I'm not talking about some spiritual vacation. I'm talking about the raw, visceral experience of your nervous system being utterly overwhelmed, of energy moving in ways it shouldn't, of reality itself feeling like it's dissolving. And yeah, breathwork can be a huge part of it - for better or worse.

You're probably thinking, "But pranayama is supposed to be good for you! It's yoga!" And you're not wrong, normally. In a stable, regulated system, pranayama can be incredibly beneficial. It can calm the mind, focus attention, and, yes, move energy. But you're not in a stable, regulated system right now, are you? You're in crisis. And in crisis, what's usually good can become dangerous. It's like giving a heart attack patient a marathon. Doesn't make sense, Right? Your system is already overstimulated, already overloaded. Adding more fuel to that fire can have devastating consequences.

I'm not here to scare you, but I'm not going to sugarcoat it either. You need the truth. You need to know what to avoid like the plague and what might, just might, offer you a sliver of relief. Because when you're in the thick of it, every little bit of information counts. Every decision you make about your body and your mind can either push you further into the abyss or pull you back, even just a little, towards solid ground.

The Dangers: What to Absolutely Avoid

Look, if you're experiencing a kundalini crisis - and by that, I mean intense involuntary movements, extreme anxiety, panic attacks, sensory overload, visions, auditory hallucinations, feeling like you're vibrating uncontrollably, or just an overwhelming sense of dread and energetic chaos - then certain types of pranayama are going to make it worse. A lot worse. I've seen it, I've lived it, and I've heard countless stories from others who made the mistake of pushing too hard. Your system is already trying to process an immense amount of energy. You don't need to add more.

Intense, Stimulating Breathwork

Here's the thing: anything that's designed to generate heat, forcefully move energy, or rapidly change your state of consciousness is a hard no. You're already generating too much heat, already moving too much energy, and your state of consciousness is already, shall we say, "altered."

  • Bhastrika (Bellows Breath): This one is a big NO. It's all about rapid, forceful inhalations and exhalations. It's designed to build heat and activate energy. You've got enough heat, believe me. You're probably already feeling like you're burning from the inside out. Doing Bhastrika will just fan those flames, potentially leading to more intense tremors, panic, and a feeling of being completely overwhelmed. I tried it once, early on, thinking it would "clear" things. It didn't. It sent me into a full-blown panic attack that lasted for hours. Never again.
  • Kapalabhati (Skull Shining Breath): Another hard pass. Similar to Bhastrika, this involves forceful exhalations driven by abdominal contractions. It's meant to purify and energize. For you right now, it's just going to overstimulate an already overstimulated nervous system. You'll feel even more wired, even more anxious, and potentially trigger more involuntary movements or energetic surges that you absolutely don't want.
  • Sudarshan Kriya: While often taught in a structured environment, if you're in crisis, any rapid, rhythmic breathing with retentions is a risk. This practice, often associated with Art of Living, involves a specific cyclical breathing pattern. It's powerful. Too powerful for a system in distress. It can significantly increase the energetic movements you're already struggling with, pushing you further into dysregulation.
  • Breath of Fire: This is a Kundalini Yoga staple, known for its rapid, continuous, and forceful breath. It's meant to build inner fire and cleanse. For someone in crisis, it's like throwing gasoline on a fire. You don't need more fire; you need to dampen it. Avoid this one at all costs.
  • Forceful Retentions (Kumbhaka): Holding your breath, especially after a deep inhale, can be incredibly activating. It builds pressure, concentrates energy, and can trigger intense internal experiences. If you're already struggling with energetic surges, this is going to make it worse. Your system is already holding onto too much; you need to release, not retain.

I know it sounds counterintuitive if you've been taught that these are "advanced" practices. But in this state, they're not advanced; they're dangerous. You're not trying to push boundaries or awaken anything further. You're trying to survive, to stabilize, to bring some semblance of peace back to your shattered reality. Know what I mean?

You might be thinking, "But what about Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link)? Doesn't it talk about intense experiences?" Yes, it does. And it talks about how you need to approach these things with extreme caution, often with professional guidance. That book, and others like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link), really emphasize how delicate this process is. You're not going to find any recommendations for forced breathwork in a crisis in those pages. Truth is, you're in an emergency. Treat it like one.

The Helpers: What Might Offer Relief

Alright, so we've covered what to avoid. Now, let's talk about what might actually help. The goal here isn't to blast open your chakras or accelerate your spiritual journey. The goal is grounding, calming, and regulating your nervous system. It's about bringing you back into your body, into the present moment, and out of the chaotic energetic whirlwind that's consuming you.

The key principle here is gentle. Everything needs to be slow, soft, and focused on exhalation. You want to activate your parasympathetic nervous system - your "rest and digest" system - not rev up your sympathetic, "fight or flight" response, which is already working overtime.

Calming, Grounding Breathwork

These techniques are about slowing down, extending your exhale, and finding a sense of safety within your own body. They won't make the kundalini go away, but they can help manage the symptoms and give you a bit of breathing room - literally.

  • Diaphragmatic Breathing (Belly Breathing): This is your absolute best friend. Forget all the fancy stuff. Just lie down, put one hand on your chest and one on your belly. As you inhale, let your belly rise. As you exhale, let it fall. Don't force it. Just breathe deeply into your belly. The key here is to make your exhale longer than your inhale. Try inhaling for a count of 4, holding for 1, and exhaling for a count of 6 or 8. This signals to your nervous system that you're safe. It's basic, it's simple, and it's incredibly effective for calming the system. Do this for 5-10 minutes, several times a day. Or whenever you feel a surge coming on.
  • Nadi Shodhana (Alternate Nostril Breathing) - without retention: This can be helpful, but you MUST omit the retentions. The goal is balance, not activation. Close your right nostril with your thumb, inhale slowly through your left. Close your left nostril with your ring finger, release your thumb, and exhale slowly through your right. Inhale through your right, close it, and exhale through your left. Keep it gentle. Keep the exhales longer than the inhales. If it feels too stimulating, even without retention, stop immediately. Your intuition is your guide here.
  • Humming Breath (Bhramari Pranayama) - gentle version: This can be incredibly soothing. Sit comfortably, close your eyes, and gently plug your ears with your thumbs or fingers. As you exhale, make a low, soft humming sound. Feel the vibration in your head and chest. This vibration can be very grounding and calming. It's like a lullaby for your nervous system. Don't force the hum; let it be gentle and sustained.
  • Sitali/Sitkari (Cooling Breaths): If you're experiencing internal heat, these can offer some relief.
    • For Sitali, curl your tongue into a tube and inhale through it, as if sipping through a straw. Exhale through your nose.
    • If you can't curl your tongue, try Sitkari: lightly clench your teeth, separate your lips, and inhale through the gaps in your teeth, making a hissing sound. Exhale through your nose.
    These are designed to literally cool your body. Again, keep it gentle. Don't force anything.
  • Box Breathing (Square Breathing): This is another excellent technique for nervous system regulation. Inhale for a count of 4, hold your breath for a count of 4, exhale for a count of 4, hold your breath for a count of 4. Then repeat. The equal counts create a sense of rhythm and stability. If the holds feel too intense, shorten them or even remove them, focusing on equal inhale and exhale. The goal is regulation, not pushing limits.

The common thread through all these helpers is gentleness, regulation, and a focus on lengthening the exhale. You're not trying to move energy; you're trying to soothe your system. You're trying to create a safe harbor in the storm. Does that land?

You know, when I was going through it, I'd often just focus on the simple act of breathing, without any specific technique. Just watching my breath, trying to make it a little longer, a little smoother. Sometimes, that was all I could manage. And it was enough. It was a lifeline. You can learn more about managing these states in books like The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) or The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link). They'll tell you the same thing: gentle, gentle, gentle. No heroics.

Beyond Breath: Other Calming Practices

While breathwork is the focus here, don't forget about other things that can help you ground. Because honestly, when kundalini is active, you need all the grounding you can get. Your nervous system is screaming for stability.

  • Earthing/Grounding: Get outside. Put your bare feet on the grass, dirt, or sand. Even just sitting under a tree can help. There's something about connecting with the earth that can literally help discharge excess energy and bring you back into your body.
  • Cold Water Exposure: A cold shower, splashing cold water on your face, or even just holding ice in your hands can be incredibly regulating. It activates the vagus nerve and can quickly calm an overactive nervous system. Start small, don't shock yourself, but notice the immediate shift it can create.
  • Gentle Movement: Think walking, gentle stretching, restorative yoga (NOT power yoga). Movements that are slow, deliberate, and help you feel your body without pushing it. Avoid anything that feels like it's building heat or intensity.
  • Sound and Vibration: Low, humming sounds, certain types of calming music, or even just listening to nature sounds can help. Avoid loud, jarring noises.
  • Routine and Structure: When your inner world is chaos, having some outer structure can be a lifesaver. Try to eat at regular times, go to bed at a similar time, and build in predictable, calming activities.
  • Diet: Avoid stimulants - caffeine, sugar, anything that makes you feel more wired. Focus on grounding foods - root vegetables, whole grains, warm, cooked meals.

So yeah, it's not just about what you do with your breath. It's about creating an entire environment - internal and external - that supports healing and stabilization. You're trying to build a sanctuary for yourself, even if it's just a small corner of your room, or a few minutes of quiet breathing.

Listen to Your Body. Seriously.

This is probably the most important piece of advice I can give you: listen to your body. Your body is giving you constant feedback. If a breath technique makes you feel more anxious, more agitated, more wired, or triggers more intense energetic sensations, STOP. Immediately. It doesn't matter what some guru said, or what you read in a book. If it feels wrong for you, right now, it is wrong. Your system is in a delicate state, and you need to treat it with extreme care and respect.

I can't emphasize this enough. There's no "pushing through" this. There's no "getting past" it by forcing yourself to do something that feels harmful. You're not trying to conquer kundalini; you're trying to integrate it, to allow it to move in a way that doesn't feel like you're being torn apart. And that means being incredibly gentle and responsive to your own internal signals.

I've learned this the hard way, pushing myself when I should have rested, trying to "fix" myself with more energy work when what I needed was less. It just made everything worse. So, please, learn from my mistakes. Be kind to yourself. Be patient. This isn't a race. It's a marathon, and sometimes, it feels like you're crawling through broken glass. But you're not alone in this. You're really not. There are others who've walked this path, and some of us have made it through to the other side. You can too.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
